meta.title: React
meta.description: React component design, hooks, state management, and frontend concerns

items.text: Are components small and focused on a single responsibility?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each component should do one thing well. If a component handles data fetching, business logic, and rendering, it should be split.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Small components are easier to test, reuse, and reason about. Large components with mixed concerns become maintenance nightmares that nobody wants to touch.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check component length (>200 lines is a yellow flag). Does it manage multiple unrelated pieces of state? Does the name accurately describe everything it does?
baseContent.exampleComment: This component handles form validation, API submission, and success/error display. Consider splitting into a form component and a submission handler hook.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: If you can't describe what a component does in one sentence, it's doing too much.

items.text: Is the component tree structured to minimize unnecessary re-renders?

baseContent.whatItMeans: State changes re-render the component and all its children. Structure the tree so that state lives close to where it's used, minimizing the blast radius of re-renders.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A state change at the top of the tree re-renders everything below it. Moving state down or extracting expensive subtrees prevents unnecessary work.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check where state is defined. Does it need to be that high in the tree? Could expensive children be memoized or extracted?
baseContent.exampleComment: The `isHovered` state is defined in the page component but only used in the header. Moving it into the header component would prevent the entire page from re-rendering on hover.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Push state down to where it's used. The lower the state, the smaller the re-render blast radius.

items.text: Are presentational and container concerns separated appropriately?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Separate data fetching and business logic (container/hooks) from rendering (presentational components). Presentational components receive data via props.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Presentational components are reusable and testable without mocking APIs. Container logic can be tested independently. The separation makes both easier to maintain.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if components mix API calls with JSX rendering. Could the data fetching be extracted into a custom hook?
baseContent.exampleComment: This component fetches data and renders the UI. Extracting the fetch logic into a `useUserData()` hook would make the component easier to test and reuse.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Components should either fetch data or render it, not both. Custom hooks bridge the gap.

items.text: Are compound components or render props used instead of overly complex prop drilling?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When components need to share internal state with children, compound components (like `<Select>` + `<Select.Option>`) are cleaner than drilling props through many levels.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Prop drilling through 3+ levels makes components rigid and hard to refactor. Compound components and render props provide flexibility without the coupling.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for props being passed through intermediate components unchanged. Are there components acting purely as prop pass-throughs?
baseContent.exampleComment: The `isOpen` prop is being drilled through 3 components. A compound component pattern or context would simplify this.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: If you're drilling props through components that don't use them, consider compound components or context.

items.text: Are component files co-located with their styles, tests, and types?

baseContent.whatItMeans: A component's styles, tests, and types should live next to the component file, not in distant directories.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Co-location makes components self-contained and portable. Hunting across `src/styles/`, `src/types/`, and `src/tests/` to understand one component slows everyone down.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check where tests, styles, and types for new components are placed. Are they next to the component or in a separate directory tree?
baseContent.exampleComment: The test for this component is in `__tests__/` at the root. Moving it to `components/UserCard/UserCard.test.tsx` would make it easier to find and maintain.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep related files together. A component folder with its styles, tests, and types is the gold standard.

items.text: Are default exports avoided in favor of named exports for better refactoring support?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Named exports (`export function UserCard`) are preferred over default exports (`export default function`) for better IDE support and refactoring.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Named exports enable auto-import, make renaming easier to track across the codebase, and prevent files from being imported under inconsistent names.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for `export default`. Does the project convention allow it? Named exports make grep/find-references more reliable.
baseContent.exampleComment: Consider using a named export here. Default exports can be imported under any name, which makes it harder to track usage across the codebase.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Named exports are easier to rename, search for, and auto-import. Default exports invite inconsistency.

items.text: Are prop types well-defined with TypeScript interfaces?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every component's props should have a TypeScript interface that documents the component's API.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Typed props catch misuse at compile time, serve as documentation, and enable IDE autocomplete for consumers of the component.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that new components have a named props interface. Are props typed precisely (not `any` or overly broad)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This component's props are typed inline. Extracting to a named `UserCardProps` interface makes the API clearer and the type reusable.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: A props interface is the component's contract. Make it explicit and precise.

items.text: Are required vs optional props distinguished correctly?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Required props should not have `?`. Optional props should. Default values should be provided for optional props where sensible.
baseContent.whyItMatters: When everything is optional, consumers don't know what's actually needed. When required props are missing, the compiler catches it immediately.
baseContent.howToVerify: Review the props interface. Are truly required props marked as required? Do optional props have sensible defaults?
baseContent.exampleComment: The `userId` prop is optional but the component crashes without it. This should be required so TypeScript catches missing usage.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Required means required. Don't make everything optional out of convenience.

items.text: Are callback props named with `on` prefix (`onClick`, `onSubmit`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Callback props should follow the `onEventName` convention (e.g., `onClick`, `onSubmit`, `onChange`). Handler functions inside the component use `handleEventName`.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Consistent naming makes component APIs predictable. Developers immediately recognize `on*` as callbacks and `handle*` as internal handlers.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check callback props in the interface. Do they follow `on` + verb naming? Are internal handlers named `handle` + verb?
baseContent.exampleComment: This callback prop is named `clickAction`. Following React conventions, `onClick` would be more intuitive for consumers.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: on* for props (external API), handle* for internal handlers. Consistency is the convention.

items.text: Is state kept as local as possible (lifted only when necessary)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: State should live in the lowest component that needs it. Only lift state up when siblings need to share it.
baseContent.whyItMatters: State too high in the tree causes unnecessary re-renders and makes components harder to reason about. Local state is easier to manage and test.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check where useState is called. Is the state used only in this component and its children? Could it be pushed lower?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `isExpanded` state is in the parent but only the accordion component uses it. Moving it into the accordion keeps the parent simpler.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Start local. Lift state only when you must. The lower the state, the simpler the component.

items.text: Is derived state computed during render, not stored in state?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Values computable from existing state or props should be calculated during render, not stored in separate state variables.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Derived state stored in useState creates sync bugs — you must remember to update it whenever the source changes. Computing during render is always in sync.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for useState where the value is derived from props or other state. Is there a useEffect that syncs it? That's a red flag.
baseContent.exampleComment: The `fullName` state is derived from `firstName` + `lastName`. Computing it during render (`const fullName = firstName + ' ' + lastName`) eliminates the sync issue.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code:
const [items, setItems] = useState<Item[]>([]);
const filteredItems = items.filter(i => i.active); // computed inline

baseContent.keyTakeaway: If you can compute it from existing state, don't store it. Derived state = no state.

items.text: Are controlled vs uncontrolled components chosen intentionally?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Controlled components have their state managed by React (via props + onChange). Uncontrolled components manage their own state internally (using refs).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Mixing controlled and uncontrolled patterns in the same component causes bugs. The choice should be intentional based on whether the parent needs to control the value.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check form inputs. Are they controlled (value + onChange) or uncontrolled (defaultValue + ref)? Is the choice consistent and intentional?
baseContent.exampleComment: This input has both `value` and `defaultValue` set — it's mixing controlled and uncontrolled patterns. Pick one approach.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
<input
  value={name} // controlled
  defaultValue="John" // also uncontrolled??
  onChange={e => setName(e.target.value)}
/>

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Pick controlled or uncontrolled. Don't mix them on the same input.

items.text: Is state shape flat and normalized (no deeply nested state objects)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: State objects should be flat. Deeply nested state is hard to update immutably and easy to get wrong.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Updating nested state requires careful spreading at every level. Missing one level silently drops data. Flat state is simpler to update and less error-prone.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check useState calls for deeply nested objects (3+ levels). Could the state be flattened or split into separate state variables?
baseContent.exampleComment: This state has 4 levels of nesting. Flattening to separate state variables or normalizing with IDs would make updates much simpler and safer.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Flat state is easy to update. Nested state is a bug factory. Flatten or normalize.

items.text: Are hooks called at the top level only (not inside conditions, loops, or nested functions)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Hooks must be called in the same order every render. They cannot be inside conditions, loops, or nested functions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: React tracks hooks by call order. Conditional hooks break this tracking, causing state to shift between hooks and produce bizarre bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for hooks inside if/else blocks, loops, or early returns. The eslint rule `react-hooks/rules-of-hooks` should catch this.
baseContent.exampleComment: This `useState` is inside an if block. Hooks must be called at the top level, in the same order every render. Move it above the condition.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: All hooks at the top, always. No conditions, no loops, no early returns before hooks.

items.text: Are `useEffect` dependency arrays correct and complete?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every value from the component scope used inside useEffect must be in the dependency array, or the effect will use stale values.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Missing dependencies cause stale closure bugs. Extra dependencies cause unnecessary re-runs. Both lead to hard-to-diagnose issues.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check each useEffect's dependency array. Are all referenced state/props/functions listed? Does the eslint exhaustive-deps rule pass?
baseContent.exampleComment: This effect references `userId` but it's not in the dependency array. The effect won't re-run when `userId` changes, using the stale initial value instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: The dependency array is a contract: 'run this effect when these values change.' Get it right.

items.text: Are cleanup functions returned from `useEffect` where needed (subscriptions, timers, event listeners)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Effects that create subscriptions, timers, or event listeners must return a cleanup function to avoid memory leaks and zombie listeners.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without cleanup, subscriptions pile up on re-renders, timers fire after unmount, and event listeners accumulate — causing memory leaks and bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check useEffect calls that create subscriptions, setInterval, setTimeout, or addEventListener. Do they return a cleanup function?
baseContent.exampleComment: This useEffect sets up a WebSocket connection but doesn't clean it up. When the component re-renders or unmounts, the old connection stays open. Return a cleanup function.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: If your effect creates something, your cleanup should destroy it. Subscribe → unsubscribe. Listen → unlisten.

items.text: Is `useEffect` not used for things computable from state/props (use `useMemo` or compute inline)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Don't use useEffect to 'sync' derived values. If a value can be computed from state or props, compute it during render.
baseContent.whyItMatters: useEffect for derived values causes an extra render cycle: render → effect fires → setState → render again. Computing inline eliminates this unnecessary double render.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for useEffect that just transforms state/props into new state. Could the computation happen directly in the render body or useMemo?
baseContent.exampleComment: This useEffect computes `fullName` from `first` + `last` and stores it in state. Just compute it inline: `const fullName = first + ' ' + last`. No effect needed.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: You probably don't need useEffect for that. Compute derived values inline.

items.text: Are `useMemo` and `useCallback` used appropriately (not prematurely, but where re-renders are costly)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: useMemo caches expensive computations. useCallback caches function references. Both should be used when re-renders are measurably costly, not as a default.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Premature memoization adds complexity without benefit — the overhead of tracking dependencies can exceed the cost of just re-computing. But missing memoization where needed causes jank.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if useMemo/useCallback is used everywhere (probably over-optimizing) or nowhere despite expensive re-renders (probably under-optimizing).
baseContent.exampleComment: This useMemo wraps a simple string concatenation. The overhead of memoization exceeds the cost of the computation. Reserve useMemo for genuinely expensive operations.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Memoize expensive computations and callbacks passed to memoized children. Skip it for cheap operations.

items.text: Are `useRef` values not used in dependency arrays (they don't trigger re-renders)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: useRef returns a mutable object whose `.current` property can change without triggering re-renders. Putting refs in dependency arrays does nothing.
baseContent.whyItMatters: The ref object identity is stable across renders, so it will never trigger the effect to re-run. This gives a false sense that the effect tracks the ref value.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check useEffect dependency arrays for ref objects. If the effect needs to react to ref value changes, a different pattern (state, callback ref) is needed.
baseContent.exampleComment: Including `timerRef` in the dependency array has no effect — ref objects are stable across renders. The effect won't re-run when `timerRef.current` changes.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Refs are for mutable values that don't trigger re-renders. Don't put them in dependency arrays.

items.text: Are custom hooks extracted for reusable stateful logic?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When multiple components share the same stateful logic (data fetching, form handling, subscriptions), extract it into a custom hook.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Custom hooks eliminate duplication of stateful logic. Without them, the same useState + useEffect pattern gets copy-pasted across components.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for similar useState + useEffect patterns repeated across components. Could they be extracted into a shared hook?
baseContent.exampleComment: This fetch + loading + error pattern appears in 3 components. A `useAsync()` or `useFetch()` hook would eliminate the duplication.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: If two components share stateful logic, extract a custom hook. If three share it, you definitely should.

items.text: Are custom hooks prefixed with `use`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Custom hooks must start with `use` (e.g., `useAuth`, `useFetch`). This is how React identifies hooks and enforces the rules of hooks.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without the `use` prefix, React's linter can't enforce hook rules (top-level calls, dependency arrays). The function won't be treated as a hook.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that any new function containing hooks is prefixed with `use`. Functions without the prefix that call hooks will cause linting issues.
baseContent.exampleComment: This `fetchData` function calls `useState` and `useEffect` but isn't prefixed with `use`. Rename to `useFetchData` so React's linter can enforce hook rules.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: If it calls hooks, it must start with `use`. No exceptions.

items.text: Is `useState` used for values that should trigger a re-render, `useRef` for values that shouldn't?

baseContent.whatItMeans: useState triggers re-renders when updated. useRef stores mutable values without triggering re-renders. Choose based on whether the UI depends on the value.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Using useState for values the UI doesn't display (timers, previous values, DOM references) causes unnecessary re-renders. Using useRef for displayed values means the UI won't update.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if useState values are used in JSX. If not, could they be refs? Check if ref values are displayed — they should probably be state.
baseContent.exampleComment: This `intervalId` is stored in useState but it's never rendered — it's just used for cleanup. A useRef would avoid unnecessary re-renders when the timer starts.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: useState for values the UI shows. useRef for values the UI doesn't care about.

items.text: Are `key` props stable, unique, and not using array indices (unless list is static)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Keys tell React which items changed, were added, or removed. They must be stable (don't change between renders) and unique among siblings.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Using array indices as keys causes items to lose state when the list is reordered, filtered, or items are inserted. This creates subtle UI bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for `key={index}` or `key={i}` in JSX map calls. Does the list ever get reordered or filtered? If so, use a stable identifier.
baseContent.exampleComment: Using the array index as key here will cause items to lose their input state when the list is reordered. Use `item.id` instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use stable, unique IDs as keys. Array indices are only safe for static, never-reordered lists.

items.text: Are conditional renders clean (ternary for simple, early return for complex)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Simple conditions use ternaries inline. Complex conditions (multiple states, loading/error/success) use early returns for clarity.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Nested ternaries in JSX are unreadable. Early returns for complex conditions keep the happy path clean and each state easy to understand.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for nested ternaries in JSX (more than one level deep). Would early returns be clearer? Check for complex inline conditions.
baseContent.exampleComment: This triple-nested ternary is hard to follow. Consider using early returns for loading and error states, keeping the main JSX clean.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: One level of ternary is fine. Two or more? Use early returns.

items.text: Is `&&` rendering guarded against falsy values rendering `0` or `""` (`count && <X/>` bug)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The `&&` pattern in JSX renders the right side if the left is truthy. But `0` and `''` are falsy yet still render in React — `0 && <Component/>` renders `0`.
baseContent.whyItMatters: This is one of React's most common gotchas. A stray `0` appearing on screen is confusing and hard to debug if you don't know about this behavior.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for `{count && <Component/>}` or `{items.length && <List/>}` patterns. When count is 0 or length is 0, React renders `0` on screen.
baseContent.exampleComment: When `items.length` is 0, this will render the number `0` on screen instead of nothing. Use `items.length > 0 &&` or a ternary instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
{items.length && <ItemList items={items} />}
// When items.length === 0, renders '0' on screen

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never use a number as the left side of && in JSX. Convert to boolean first.

items.text: Are fragments (`<>...</>`) used instead of unnecessary wrapper `<div>`s?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When a component returns multiple elements, use React Fragments (`<>...</>`) instead of wrapping in a `<div>` that adds an extra DOM node.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Extra wrapper divs break CSS layouts (flexbox, grid), add unnecessary DOM depth, and can interfere with accessibility tree structure.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for `<div>` wrappers that exist only to satisfy React's single-root requirement. Could they be replaced with fragments?
baseContent.exampleComment: This wrapper `<div>` is only here to group the elements. A fragment (`<>...</>`) would avoid the extra DOM node that's breaking the flex layout.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use fragments to group elements without adding DOM nodes. Save divs for when you actually need a wrapper.

items.text: Are large lists virtualized (react-window, react-virtuoso) when rendering 100+ items?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Lists with 100+ items should use virtualization — only rendering items visible in the viewport instead of the entire list.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Rendering 1000 DOM nodes is slow. Virtualization renders ~20 visible items, dramatically reducing memory usage and improving scroll performance.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check list renders with `.map()`. How many items could the list have? If 100+, is virtualization used? Check for scroll performance issues.
baseContent.exampleComment: This list can have 500+ items all rendered at once. At that scale, consider react-window or react-virtuoso to only render visible items.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// Renders all 1000 items at once
{items.map(item => <Row key={item.id} item={item} />)}

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Virtualize lists over 100 items. The user can only see ~20 at a time anyway.

items.text: Are inline object/array literals avoided in JSX props (creates new references each render)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Inline objects/arrays in JSX (`style={{ color: 'red' }}`, `options={[1,2,3]}`) create new references every render, potentially triggering child re-renders.
baseContent.whyItMatters: If a child component is memoized (React.memo), inline objects defeat the memoization because the reference changes every render even if the values don't.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for object/array literals directly in JSX props. Are the children memoized? If so, move the values to constants or useMemo.
baseContent.exampleComment: This `style={{ margin: 8 }}` creates a new object every render. Since `Card` is wrapped in React.memo, this defeats the memoization. Extract to a constant.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
<Card style={{ margin: 8, padding: 16 }} />
<Select options={['a', 'b', 'c']} />

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Inline objects in JSX create new references every render. Extract to constants or useMemo.

items.text: Is `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` avoided or sanitized if used?

baseContent.whatItMeans: dangerouslySetInnerHTML injects raw HTML into the DOM. If the HTML comes from user input, it's a direct XSS vulnerability.
baseContent.whyItMatters: XSS attacks through unsanitized HTML can steal sessions, redirect users, and execute arbitrary code. It's one of the OWASP top 10 vulnerabilities.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` in the diff. Is the content sanitized with DOMPurify or similar? Is it from a trusted source?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` renders user-provided HTML without sanitization. This is an XSS vulnerability. Please sanitize with DOMPurify or use a markdown renderer instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// XSS vulnerability — user content rendered as raw HTML
<div dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: userComment }} />

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: If you must use dangerouslySetInnerHTML, always sanitize with DOMPurify. Prefer markdown renderers when possible.

items.text: Is React Context used for truly global state, not for prop drilling avoidance in shallow trees?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Context is for genuinely global state (theme, auth, locale). Don't use it just to avoid passing props down 2-3 levels.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Context changes re-render all consumers. Using it for frequently changing local state causes performance issues. Props are simpler for shallow trees.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new context providers. Is the state truly global? Could it be passed as props? How often does the value change?
baseContent.exampleComment: This context provides form field values that change on every keystroke. Since only the form and its direct children use it, props would be simpler and more performant.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Context for global state that changes rarely (theme, auth). Props for local state that changes often.

items.text: Are context values memoized to prevent unnecessary re-renders?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The value passed to a Context.Provider creates a new reference each render unless memoized, causing all consumers to re-render.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without memoization, every parent re-render triggers every context consumer to re-render — even if the context value hasn't actually changed.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check Context.Provider value props. Is the value wrapped in useMemo? Are callback functions wrapped in useCallback?
baseContent.exampleComment: This context value is an inline object that creates a new reference every render. Wrap it in useMemo to prevent unnecessary consumer re-renders.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always memoize context values. An unmemoized provider is a hidden performance drain.

items.text: Are context providers placed as low in the tree as possible?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Place context providers at the lowest tree level that encompasses all consumers. Don't put everything at the app root.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Providers at the root re-render the entire app on context changes. Providers closer to consumers limit the blast radius of re-renders.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check where new providers are placed. Are all consumers actually below this point in the tree? Could the provider be moved lower?
baseContent.exampleComment: This ThemeProvider is at the app root but only the settings page uses it. Moving it to wrap just the settings route would limit unnecessary re-renders.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Push providers down to where they're needed. Not everything belongs at the root.

items.text: Is the state management library (Redux, Zustand, Jotai, etc.) used consistently with project patterns?

baseContent.whatItMeans: If the project uses a state management library, new state should follow existing patterns — same file structure, naming conventions, and access patterns.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Inconsistent state management (some in Redux, some in Zustand, some in useState) makes the codebase confusing and hard to maintain.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if new state follows the project's existing state management patterns. Is the same library used? Same file organization?
baseContent.exampleComment: The rest of the app uses Zustand stores. This new feature uses Redux. Could we use Zustand to keep things consistent?
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Follow existing state management patterns. Consistency across the codebase matters more than library preference.

items.text: Are selectors used to read only necessary slices of state?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Components should subscribe to only the state slices they need, not the entire store. This prevents re-renders when unrelated state changes.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Selecting the entire store means every state change re-renders the component. Selecting just the needed slice means re-renders only when relevant data changes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check store subscriptions. Is the component selecting more state than it uses? Could a more targeted selector reduce re-renders?
baseContent.exampleComment: This component selects the entire user store but only uses `user.name`. Use `useStore(s => s.user.name)` to avoid re-rendering when other user fields change.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: const store = useStore(); // subscribes to everything return {store.user.name};

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code: const name = useStore(s => s.user.name); // only re-renders when name changes return {name};

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Select the minimum state needed. Granular selectors = fewer re-renders.

items.text: Are actions/mutations named clearly and consistently?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Store actions should have clear, descriptive names that express what they do (e.g., `addItem`, `removeUser`, `toggleSidebar`).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Clear action names make the codebase self-documenting. When debugging, you can understand state changes from the action names alone.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check action/mutation names in store definitions. Are they descriptive? Do they follow a consistent naming pattern?
baseContent.exampleComment: This action is named `update`. Update what? `updateUserProfile` or `setUserName` would be much clearer when debugging state changes.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Name actions after what they do to the state. Your future self debugging at 2am will thank you.

items.text: Are styles scoped to components (CSS modules, styled-components, Tailwind)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Styles should be scoped to their component so they don't leak and affect other components. Use CSS modules, styled-components, or Tailwind.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Global CSS causes unexpected style collisions. A change in one component's styles can break another component's appearance across the app.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if new styles are scoped. Are class names unique (via CSS modules or BEM)? Or are global selectors being added?
baseContent.exampleComment: This global `.button` class will affect every button in the app. Use a CSS module or scoped class name to limit the blast radius.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
/* global.css — affects everything */
.button { padding: 8px; background: blue; }

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Scope styles to components. Global CSS is a maintenance nightmare at scale.

items.text: Are magic numbers in CSS extracted to design tokens or variables?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Hardcoded pixel values, colors, and spacing should come from design tokens or CSS variables for consistency.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Magic numbers make visual consistency impossible. When the design changes (e.g., primary color), you have to find-and-replace across hundreds of files.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for hardcoded color hex values, pixel sizes, and spacing that should come from the design system.
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses `#3b82f6` directly. The design system has `var(--color-primary)` for this. Using the token ensures consistency when the palette changes.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Design tokens over magic numbers. One change to the token updates everywhere.

items.text: Is responsive design considered (mobile-first, breakpoints)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: The UI should work across screen sizes. Mobile-first design starts with mobile styles and adds complexity for larger screens via media queries.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Users access apps on phones, tablets, and desktops. A non-responsive UI is unusable on devices it wasn't designed for.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if new UI components have responsive styles. Do they handle narrow screens? Are breakpoints consistent with the project's system?
baseContent.exampleComment: This layout uses fixed widths that don't work below 768px. Consider using flex/grid with responsive breakpoints for mobile support.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Design mobile-first. Add complexity for larger screens, not the other way around.

items.text: Are dark mode / theme tokens used instead of hardcoded colors?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Colors should come from theme tokens that change with the active theme, not hardcoded hex values that break in dark mode.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Hardcoded white text works in dark mode but is invisible in light mode. Theme tokens ensure colors adapt to the active theme automatically.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for hardcoded color values in new styles. Would they work in both light and dark mode? Should they use theme tokens?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `color: #ffffff` works in dark mode but will be invisible in light mode. Use `var(--text-primary)` instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: .header { color: #ffffff; background: #1a1a2e; }

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code: .header { color: var(--text-primary); background: var(--bg-surface); }

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Theme tokens for all colors. Never hardcode values that break when the theme changes.

items.text: Are CSS animations using `transform` and `opacity` for performance?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Animate `transform` and `opacity` properties. They run on the GPU compositor thread, avoiding layout recalculation and paint.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Animating `width`, `height`, `top`, `left`, or `margin` triggers layout recalculation on every frame, causing jank. Transform and opacity skip this entirely.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check CSS animations and transitions. Are they animating compositable properties (transform, opacity) or layout properties (width, height, top)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This animation changes `left` on every frame, triggering layout recalculation. Use `transform: translateX()` instead for smooth 60fps animation.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Animate transform and opacity only. Everything else causes layout thrashing.

items.text: Do interactive elements have accessible names (labels, aria-label)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Buttons, links, and inputs need accessible names that screen readers announce. Use visible labels, aria-label, or aria-labelledby.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without accessible names, screen reader users hear 'button' with no context about what it does. This makes the app unusable for visually impaired users.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check interactive elements for labels. Icon-only buttons need aria-label. Inputs need associated labels. Links need descriptive text (not 'click here').
baseContent.exampleComment: This icon button has no accessible name. Add `aria-label="Close dialog"` so screen readers can announce what it does.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: <button onClick={onClose}>✕</button> {/* screen reader: 'button' */}

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code: <button onClick={onClose} aria-label="Close dialog">✕</button>

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every interactive element needs a name. If there's no visible text, add aria-label.

items.text: Are semantic HTML elements used (`<button>`, `<nav>`, `<main>`, not `<div onClick>`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use native HTML elements for their intended purpose: `<button>` for actions, `<a>` for navigation, `<nav>` for navigation menus, `<main>` for main content.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Semantic elements have built-in keyboard support, ARIA roles, and screen reader behavior. A `<div onClick>` has none of these — it's invisible to assistive technology.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for `<div onClick>` or `<span onClick>` that should be buttons. Check for proper landmark elements (nav, main, header, footer).
baseContent.exampleComment: This `<div onClick>` should be a `<button>`. Buttons are focusable, respond to Enter/Space, and are announced by screen readers. Divs need manual ARIA work.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: Submit

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code: <button onClick={handleSubmit} className="btn">Submit</button>

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use the right element for the job. Buttons for actions, anchors for links, inputs for data entry.

items.text: Is keyboard navigation supported (focus management, tab order)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: All interactive features must be usable with keyboard only. Focus should be visible and follow a logical tab order.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Users who can't use a mouse (motor impairments, screen reader users, power users) rely on keyboard navigation. If it doesn't work, the app is inaccessible.
baseContent.howToVerify: Tab through the new UI without a mouse. Can you reach and activate every interactive element? Is focus visible? Does tab order make sense?
baseContent.exampleComment: This dropdown menu can only be opened with a click. Adding keyboard support (Enter to open, Arrow keys to navigate, Escape to close) would make it accessible.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Tab through your UI. If you can't use it without a mouse, neither can many users.

items.text: Are ARIA roles and attributes used correctly (not redundant with semantic HTML)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: ARIA attributes supplement HTML semantics. They should not be redundant with native semantics (e.g., `role="button"` on a `<button>`).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Redundant ARIA adds noise for screen readers. Incorrect ARIA is worse than no ARIA — it misleads assistive technology about the element's purpose.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check ARIA attributes in the diff. Are they necessary? Does the native element already provide the semantics? Is the ARIA usage correct?
baseContent.exampleComment: This `<button role="button">` is redundant — buttons already have the button role. Remove the explicit role. ARIA is only needed when native semantics are insufficient.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
<button role="button">Submit</button>
<nav role="navigation">...</nav>

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
{/* inherent button role */}
...
{/* inherent navigation role */} {/* ARIA needed: custom widget */}
...

baseContent.keyTakeaway: First rule of ARIA: don't use ARIA if native HTML provides the semantics.

items.text: Are color contrast ratios sufficient (WCAG AA minimum)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Text must have enough contrast against its background: 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text (WCAG AA).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Low contrast text is hard to read for everyone and impossible for users with low vision. It's one of the most common accessibility failures.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new color combinations with a contrast checker. Light gray text on white is a common failure. Dark mode colors need checking too.
baseContent.exampleComment: This light gray text (#999) on white background has a contrast ratio of 2.8:1, below the 4.5:1 WCAG AA minimum. Darken to at least #767676.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: 4.5:1 contrast minimum for text. Use a contrast checker — your eyes aren't reliable enough.

items.text: Are loading and error states announced to screen readers (aria-live regions)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Dynamic content changes (loading spinners, error messages, success alerts) should be announced to screen readers via aria-live regions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Screen reader users don't see visual changes. Without aria-live, they won't know that data is loading, an error occurred, or a form was submitted successfully.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check loading and error states. Are they in aria-live regions? Is the politeness level appropriate (polite for non-urgent, assertive for errors)?
baseContent.exampleComment: This error message appears visually but isn't in an aria-live region. Screen reader users won't know the form submission failed. Add `aria-live="assertive"`.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
{error &&
{error}
}

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code: {error && {error}}

baseContent.keyTakeaway: If the UI changes dynamically, announce it with aria-live. Screen readers can't see your spinners.

items.text: Are form inputs associated with labels (`htmlFor` / `id`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every form input needs a programmatically associated label via `htmlFor`/`id` pairing or by wrapping the input in the label element.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without associated labels, screen readers announce 'edit text' with no context. Users don't know what data to enter. Labels also make the click target larger.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check form inputs for associated labels. Does each input have a label with matching `htmlFor`? Or is it wrapped in a `<label>` element?
baseContent.exampleComment: This input has no associated label. Add `<label htmlFor="email">Email</label>` and `id="email"` on the input.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: Email {/* no label association */}

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
<label htmlFor="email">Email</label>
<input type="email" id="email" />

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every input needs a label. htmlFor + id, or wrap the input in <label>.

items.text: Are images with content meaning given `alt` text? Are decorative images `alt=""`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Images that convey information need descriptive alt text. Decorative images (icons, backgrounds) should have empty alt (`alt=""`) to be skipped by screen readers.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without alt text, screen readers announce the filename ('IMG_2847.jpg') which is meaningless. Decorative images without `alt=""` add noise to the screen reader experience.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check `` tags. Do content images have descriptive alt text? Do decorative images have `alt=""`? Are any alt texts missing entirely?
baseContent.exampleComment: This product image is missing alt text. Screen readers will announce the filename. Add descriptive alt text like `alt="Blue running shoes, side view"`.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: {/* no alt — screen reader says filename */} {/* announced unnecessarily */}

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code: {/* skipped by screen reader */}

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Content images get descriptive alt text. Decorative images get alt="". Never omit alt entirely.

items.text: Are images optimized (WebP/AVIF, lazy loading, responsive `srcSet`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Images should use modern formats (WebP, AVIF), lazy load below the fold, and provide responsive sizes via srcSet.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Images are typically the largest page payload. Unoptimized images cause slow page loads, wasted bandwidth, and poor Core Web Vitals scores.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check image elements. Are modern formats used? Are below-the-fold images lazy loaded? Are responsive sizes provided for different viewports?
baseContent.exampleComment: These images are full-size PNGs loaded eagerly. Using WebP with lazy loading and responsive srcSet could cut page weight by 60%+.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: {/* 2MB PNG, loaded eagerly */}

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Optimize images: modern format, lazy loading, responsive sizes. It's usually the biggest performance win.

items.text: Are large dependencies evaluated for bundle size impact?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Before adding a new dependency, check its bundle size impact. A utility library should not add 200KB to your bundle.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Large dependencies slow down page load for every user. Many packages have lighter alternatives (e.g., date-fns vs moment, lodash-es vs lodash).
baseContent.howToVerify: Check new dependencies on bundlephobia.com. What's the gzipped size? Does it tree-shake? Is there a lighter alternative?
baseContent.exampleComment: This adds `moment.js` (72KB gzipped) for one date formatting call. `date-fns/format` would add ~2KB for the same functionality.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Check bundle size before adding dependencies. Small alternatives often exist.

items.text: Is code splitting used for routes and heavy components (`React.lazy`, dynamic `import()`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Split the bundle by route and by heavy component so users only download the code they need for the current page.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without code splitting, users download the entire app on first load — including pages they may never visit. This slows initial load significantly.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if routes use lazy loading. Are heavy components (charts, editors, maps) dynamically imported? Does the bundle analyzer show a reasonable split?
baseContent.exampleComment: The admin dashboard is eagerly loaded but only ~5% of users visit it. Use `React.lazy()` to split it into a separate chunk that loads on demand.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: import AdminDashboard from './AdminDashboard'; // loaded for everyone

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
const AdminDashboard = React.lazy(() => import('./AdminDashboard'));

<Suspense fallback={<Loading />}>
  <AdminDashboard />
</Suspense>

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Split by route at minimum. Lazy-load heavy components. Users shouldn't download code they don't need.

items.text: Are network requests deduplicated and cached (React Query, SWR, Apollo)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use a data fetching library (React Query, SWR) that automatically deduplicates in-flight requests and caches responses.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without request deduplication, 5 components requesting the same user data make 5 API calls. Caching avoids refetching data that hasn't changed.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for duplicate fetch calls. Are multiple components fetching the same data independently? Is there a caching strategy?
baseContent.exampleComment: Three components each call `fetchUser(id)` independently. Using React Query with a shared query key would deduplicate these into a single request.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use a data fetching library that deduplicates and caches. Don't roll your own.

items.text: Are web vitals considered (LCP, FID/INP, CLS)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Core Web Vitals measure real user experience: LCP (loading), INP (interactivity), CLS (visual stability). Changes should not regress these metrics.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Web Vitals affect SEO ranking and user experience. A page that loads fast but has layout shifts feels broken. A page that's slow to respond to clicks feels laggy.
baseContent.howToVerify: Consider if the change impacts LCP (large images, fonts), INP (heavy handlers, long tasks), or CLS (dynamic content shifting layout).
baseContent.exampleComment: This dynamically inserted ad banner will cause layout shift (CLS). Reserve the space with a fixed-height placeholder to prevent content from jumping.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Think about loading (LCP), responsiveness (INP), and visual stability (CLS) for every UI change.

items.text: Are expensive computations moved to web workers or debounced/throttled?

baseContent.whatItMeans: CPU-intensive tasks (parsing large files, complex calculations) should run off the main thread or be debounced/throttled to prevent UI freezing.
baseContent.whyItMatters: The main thread handles rendering and user input. Long-running computations block it, causing the UI to freeze and creating poor INP scores.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for expensive synchronous operations in event handlers or render paths. Could they be debounced, throttled, or moved to a web worker?
baseContent.exampleComment: This search filter runs a complex regex on 10,000 items on every keystroke, blocking the main thread. Debouncing the input (300ms) would keep the UI responsive.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Debounce input handlers. Move heavy computation off the main thread. Keep the UI responsive.

items.text: Are fonts loaded efficiently (preload, `font-display: swap`)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Web fonts should be preloaded for critical text, use `font-display: swap` to show fallback text immediately, and minimize the number of font files loaded.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Fonts are render-blocking by default. Without `font-display: swap`, users see invisible text until the font loads (FOIT). Without preloading, fonts load late.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check font loading strategy. Is font-display set? Are critical fonts preloaded? Are unused font weights/styles loaded?
baseContent.exampleComment: This loads 8 font weights but only uses 2. Reducing to the weights actually used would save ~200KB and speed up rendering.

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
<link rel="preload" href="/fonts/inter-400.woff2" as="font" type="font/woff2" crossorigin>

<style>
  @font-face {
    font-family: 'Inter';
    src: url('/fonts/inter-400.woff2') format('woff2');
    font-display: swap;
  }
</style>

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Preload critical fonts, use font-display: swap, and only load the weights you use.

items.text: Are API calls handled with loading, error, and empty states?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every API call should handle three states: loading (show a spinner), error (show an error message with retry), and empty (show an empty state).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unhandled states create confusing UX: blank screens while loading, silent failures on errors, and confusion when there's no data.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check components that fetch data. Do they handle loading, error, and empty states? Are error messages actionable?
baseContent.exampleComment: This component renders nothing while data is loading — the user sees a blank screen. Add a loading state so they know the app is working.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
const { data } = useFetch('/api/users');
return <UserList users={data} />; // undefined during load

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every data fetch needs loading, error, and empty states. No blank screens, no silent failures.

items.text: Is optimistic UI used for instant-feeling mutations where appropriate?

baseContent.whatItMeans: For mutations the user expects to succeed (liking, toggling, adding to cart), update the UI immediately and reconcile with the server response.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Waiting for a server response to show a like/unlike feels laggy. Optimistic updates make the app feel instant while handling errors gracefully.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check mutation handlers. Does the UI wait for the server response? Would the interaction feel better with an instant update?
baseContent.exampleComment: This like button waits 200ms for the API response before updating. Since likes rarely fail, an optimistic update would make it feel instant.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Update the UI optimistically for actions that rarely fail. Roll back on error.

items.text: Are network errors handled gracefully (retry, offline state)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Network failures should show a user-friendly message with retry option, not crash the app. Consider offline states for mobile users.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Network errors happen constantly — flaky WiFi, subway tunnels, server deploys. Users need to know what happened and how to recover.
baseContent.howToVerify: Disconnect from the network and test. Does the app show helpful error states? Can the user retry? Does it recover when reconnected?
baseContent.exampleComment: This component crashes when the API is unreachable. Adding an error boundary with a retry button would give users a way to recover.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Assume the network will fail. Show helpful errors and let users retry.

items.text: Are stale-while-revalidate patterns used for frequently accessed data?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Show cached data immediately while fetching fresh data in the background. The user sees instant content that updates seamlessly when new data arrives.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without stale-while-revalidate, every page visit starts with a loading spinner. With it, users see cached data instantly and get updates transparently.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check data fetching for frequently accessed data. Is there a caching strategy? Would stale-while-revalidate improve the user experience?
baseContent.exampleComment: The user profile page shows a spinner every time you navigate to it. Using SWR/React Query with staleTime would show cached data instantly.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Show cached data immediately, revalidate in the background. Users hate spinners.

items.text: Are local storage / session storage values namespaced and size-bounded?

baseContent.whatItMeans: localStorage and sessionStorage keys should be namespaced to prevent collisions, and values should be bounded to prevent exceeding the ~5MB limit.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Unnamespaced keys collide across features. Unbounded storage eventually hits the browser limit, causing silent write failures.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check localStorage usage. Are keys prefixed with the app name? Is there a size limit on what's stored? Are old entries cleaned up?
baseContent.exampleComment: The key `'user'` in localStorage could collide with other apps on the same domain. Use `'myapp:user'` and consider size limits for cached data.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
localStorage.setItem('user', JSON.stringify(user));
localStorage.setItem('cache', JSON.stringify(allResults)); // unbounded

codeExamples.label: Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Namespace keys, bound sizes, clean up old data. localStorage is a shared, limited resource.

items.text: Are MDX files using proper frontmatter schema with required fields

baseContent.whatItMeans: MDX files should have valid frontmatter (YAML between --- delimiters) with all required fields like title, description, date, and author consistently defined.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Frontmatter drives metadata for SEO, navigation, and content indexing. Missing or malformed fields can break builds or produce pages with missing titles and descriptions.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that every MDX file starts with a --- frontmatter block
- Verify required fields (title, description, slug) are present
- Look for a shared schema or type definition that validates frontmatter at build time
baseContent.exampleComment: This MDX file is missing the `description` field in frontmatter. Our content schema requires it for SEO meta tags. Could you add a brief description?

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
---
title: My Post
---

# Content here

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
---
title: My Post
description: A guide to setting up MDX in Next.js
date: 2024-01-15
author: team
tags: [mdx, nextjs]
---

# Content here

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Define a consistent frontmatter schema and validate it at build time to catch missing fields early.

items.text: Are JSX components imported and used correctly within MDX content

baseContent.whatItMeans: JSX components used inside MDX files must be properly imported or provided via an MDXProvider, with correct prop usage and no runtime errors.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Incorrect imports or misused components cause build failures or runtime crashes in documentation and content pages, breaking the reader's experience.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that imported components exist and are exported from their modules
- Verify props match the component's interface
- Confirm the component renders correctly in the MDX context (no hydration mismatches)
baseContent.exampleComment: The `<CodeBlock>` component is imported from `@/components/CodeBlock` but that module exports `CodeExample` instead. Could you update the import?

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good
codeExamples.code:
import { Chart } from '../components/Chart'

export const data = [10, 20, 30]

<Chart data={data} title="Monthly Sales" />

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Treat MDX component imports with the same rigor as regular JSX — verify paths, props, and runtime behavior.

items.text: Are MDX custom components (headings, code blocks, links) mapped via MDXProvider

baseContent.whatItMeans: Custom component mappings (replacing default HTML elements like h1, code, a with styled React components) should be configured via MDXProvider or the components prop.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without proper component mapping, MDX renders plain HTML elements that miss your design system's styling, syntax highlighting, and interactive features like copy-to-clipboard on code blocks.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that an MDXProvider (or useMDXComponents) wraps the MDX content
- Verify mappings exist for key elements: h1-h6, code, pre, a, img, table
- Test that code blocks render with syntax highlighting
baseContent.exampleComment: I notice code blocks in this MDX page render as plain `<pre>` tags without syntax highlighting. Could we map `pre` and `code` to our `CodeBlock` component via the MDX components config?

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
// No component mapping — plain HTML output
<MDXContent />

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
import { MDXProvider } from '@mdx-js/react'
import { Heading, CodeBlock, Link } from './mdx-components'

const components = { h1: Heading, pre: CodeBlock, a: Link }

<MDXProvider components={components}>
  <MDXContent />
</MDXProvider>

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Map MDX elements to your design system components via MDXProvider to ensure consistent styling and functionality.

items.text: Is MDX content separated from layout logic (content vs presentation)

baseContent.whatItMeans: MDX files should focus on content (text, examples, data) while layout, navigation, and page chrome live in separate layout components or templates.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Mixing layout logic into content files makes content harder to reuse, redesign, and maintain. Authors should write content without worrying about page structure.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that MDX files don't contain layout wrappers, sidebars, or navigation components
- Verify that page layout is handled by a parent component or layout template
- Ensure frontmatter-driven layout selection (e.g., `layout: docs`) is used instead of inline layout code
baseContent.exampleComment: This MDX file includes the `<DocsLayout>` wrapper and sidebar navigation directly. Could we move the layout to a template component and let the MDX focus on content only?

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
import Layout from '../layouts/DocsLayout'
import Sidebar from '../components/Sidebar'

<Layout>
  <Sidebar />
  <main>
    # My Content
    Some text here.
  </main>
</Layout>

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
---
title: My Content
layout: docs
---

# My Content

Some text here.

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Keep MDX files as pure content — delegate layout and page chrome to templates or layout components.

items.text: Are interactive elements in MDX (demos, playgrounds) lazy-loaded

baseContent.whatItMeans: Heavy interactive components embedded in MDX (code playgrounds, charts, interactive demos) should be lazy-loaded so they don't block the initial page render.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Interactive components often bundle large dependencies (Monaco editor, chart libraries). Loading them eagerly increases page weight and degrades performance, especially on content-heavy pages.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check that interactive components use dynamic import or React.lazy
- Verify a loading fallback is shown while the component loads
- Test that the page's static content renders before interactive elements hydrate
baseContent.exampleComment: The `<LiveCodeEditor>` bundles ~500KB of Monaco editor on every page load. Could we lazy-load it with `dynamic(() => import(...), { ssr: false })` so the page content renders first?

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
import LiveEditor from '../components/LiveEditor'

# Tutorial

<LiveEditor code={`console.log('hello')`} />

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
import dynamic from 'next/dynamic' const LiveEditor = dynamic(() => import('../components/LiveEditor'), { loading: () =>

Loading editor...

, ssr: false }) # Tutorial

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Lazy-load heavy interactive components in MDX to keep content pages fast and lightweight.

items.text: Are MDX files linted for broken links, missing images, and invalid JSX

baseContent.whatItMeans: MDX files should be checked by linting tools for broken internal links, missing image files, and invalid JSX syntax that would cause build or runtime errors.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Broken links and missing images create a poor reader experience and hurt SEO. Invalid JSX in MDX silently breaks pages or causes confusing build errors.
baseContent.howToVerify:
- Check if a link-checking tool (remark-lint-no-dead-urls, markdown-link-check) is configured
- Verify image paths resolve to actual files in the repository
- Confirm MDX files pass the MDX compiler without JSX syntax errors
baseContent.exampleComment: This MDX file links to `/docs/old-page` which was renamed to `/docs/updated-page` in the last restructure. Could you update the link? Consider adding `remark-lint-no-dead-urls` to catch these automatically.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Automate link and image validation in your CI pipeline to catch broken references before they reach production.

items.text: Are React components tested with Testing Library (not Enzyme)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: React Testing Library tests components the way users interact with them. Enzyme tests internal component state and implementation.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Testing Library encourages behavior-focused tests that survive refactoring. Enzyme tests often break when internal structure changes even if behavior is unchanged.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check the testing library used. Is it @testing-library/react? Are tests querying by user-visible attributes rather than component internals?
baseContent.exampleComment: This test uses Enzyme's `shallow()` and checks `wrapper.state()`. Migrating to Testing Library would make the test more resilient to refactoring.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use React Testing Library. Test from the user's perspective, not the component's internals.

items.text: Are queries using accessible selectors (`getByRole`, `getByLabelText`) not `getByTestId`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Query elements by their accessible role, label, or text — the same way screen readers and users find them. Avoid `getByTestId`.
baseContent.whyItMatters: `getByRole` and `getByLabelText` tests double as accessibility checks. If the test can't find the element, screen readers can't either.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check test queries. Are they using `getByRole`, `getByLabelText`, `getByText`? Is `getByTestId` used only as a last resort?
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses `getByTestId('submit-btn')`. Using `getByRole('button', { name: 'Submit' })` would also verify the button is accessible.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
const button = screen.getByTestId('submit-button');
const input = screen.getByTestId('email-input');

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
const button = screen.getByRole('button', { name: 'Submit' });
const input = screen.getByLabelText('Email address');

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Query by role first, label second, text third, testId last. Accessible queries are better tests.

items.text: Are user interactions simulated with `userEvent`, not `fireEvent`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use `userEvent` from @testing-library/user-event for simulating user interactions. It fires all events a real interaction would (focus, keydown, input, change, blur).
baseContent.whyItMatters: `fireEvent` dispatches a single event. `userEvent` simulates the full browser interaction, catching bugs that only manifest with the complete event sequence.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for `fireEvent.click()` or `fireEvent.change()`. These should use `userEvent.click()` and `userEvent.type()` instead.
baseContent.exampleComment: This uses `fireEvent.change()` which skips the focus and keypress events. `userEvent.type()` simulates the full typing sequence, catching more realistic bugs.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code: fireEvent.change(input, { target: { value: 'hello' } }); // skips focus, keydown, etc.

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code: await userEvent.type(input, 'hello'); // fires focus, keydown, input, change per character

baseContent.keyTakeaway: userEvent simulates real user behavior. fireEvent is a shortcut that misses real bugs.

items.text: Are async state changes awaited with `waitFor` or `findBy`?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When testing async behavior (data fetching, state updates after API calls), use `waitFor` or `findBy` queries that automatically retry until the assertion passes.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without waiting for async updates, tests either fail intermittently (race condition) or use arbitrary timeouts that slow down the test suite.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for async operations in tests. Are they using `waitFor`, `findByText`, or `findByRole`? Are there manual `setTimeout` waits?
baseContent.exampleComment: This test checks for the success message immediately after clicking submit. The message appears after an async API call. Use `await screen.findByText('Success')` to wait.

codeExamples.label: Bad
codeExamples.code:
await userEvent.click(submitButton);
expect(screen.getByText('Success')).toBeVisible(); // may not exist yet

codeExamples.label: Good
codeExamples.code:
await userEvent.click(submitButton);
expect(await screen.findByText('Success')).toBeVisible(); // waits until found

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use findBy for elements that appear async. Use waitFor for assertions that need time to become true.

items.text: Are snapshot tests avoided or used sparingly and intentionally?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Snapshot tests capture rendered output and flag any change. They're easy to create but produce brittle tests that get blindly updated.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Large snapshots break on every UI change, and developers update them without reviewing. They test the wrong thing — structure instead of behavior.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for new snapshot tests. Are they testing meaningful behavior, or just capturing structure? Would an assertion-based test be more targeted?
baseContent.exampleComment: This snapshot test captures 200 lines of HTML. Any style or text change will break it. Consider testing specific behavior assertions instead.

codeExamples.label: Bad
Good

baseContent.keyTakeaway: Snapshot tests are easy to write and easy to ignore. Prefer targeted assertions.
